title: Why your supplier secretly wants CNY

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, your supplier doesn't want your dollars. They just won't tell you. Here's why. When your USD wire lands in their bank, three things happen they hate. One. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear and convert it. Two. Their bank takes a forex fee on the conversion. Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Stop. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's a small thing we want to share with every business owner who buys from China. If you pay your Chinese supplier in US dollars, your supplier probably wishes you didn't. They likely won't say it to you, but it's true. Here's the story. You send a USD wire. It lands at their Chinese bank. The bank holds it for about 7 to 10 days. The bank then changes the dollars into Chinese yuan and takes a small fee for doing it. While your money is in the air, the rate between USD and CNY can move. If it moves the wrong way, your supplier loses money on the deal. So what does your supplier do? Simple. They add a little extra to your quote. About 2 to 5% extra. Just enough to cover the wait, the fee, and the risk. You never see this extra. It's just baked into the price you pay. Every order. So try this on your next order. Ask your supplier for the price in Chinese yuan. Not US dollars. Just see what number comes back. You'll often see a price that is 1 to 3% lower. That gap is the padding. We built WorldFirst for this exact moment. You send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. No long wait. No mess. Your supplier gets clean money. You get a cleaner price. Search WorldFirst.

Ten orders a year, that's 6,000. Five years, that's 30,000 dollars your supplier collected just to cover what your USD wire does to them on their end. Why? Because USD hitting a Chinese bank means 7 to 10 days of waiting, a forex fee on conversion, and FX risk on their books. They priced that into your quote. Listen. Ask for the CNY price. The padding has no reason to exist. Suppliers drop quotes 1 to 3% when you pay in their currency. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, 80% land in hours. No padding. No waiting. Cleaner price every order. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first reveal
scripts.original_vo: Here's the mechanism most business owners miss when they pay Chinese suppliers. Your USD wire is not a clean payment. It's a problem the supplier has to solve. When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Stop. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. No forex fee on their side. No FX risk for them. The padding comes off the next quote. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts at near-interbank rate. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

FX risk on every order while the wire is in flight. They cover all of that in a 2 to 5% buffer often baked into your quote. You see one number on the PI. They see three problems and a margin to protect. So they accept the USD and quietly mark up to compensate. Pay them in CNY. The buffer often eases. Suppliers commonly come back 1 to 3% lower. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, near interbank rate. The relationship gets cleaner.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's something your Chinese supplier probably won't say to you openly. They don't really prefer your US dollars. They tolerate them. Walk through what's happening on their side. Every USD invoice they send you is quietly priced for the headache. There's the 7 to 10 day wait at their Chinese bank while the wire clears. There's the fee the bank takes to change US dollars into Chinese yuan. And there's the rate risk on every order while your wire is in flight. They cover all of that with a small buffer. About 2 to 5%. Often baked right into your quote. You look at the invoice and you see one number. They look at it and they see three problems and a margin they need to protect. So they smile, accept the USD, and quietly mark up the quote to keep themselves whole. Here's where things change. Pay them in Chinese yuan directly. The buffer often eases up. Suppliers commonly come back with a quote that's 1 to 3% lower. That's the friction leaving the deal. That's what we made WorldFirst for. We send Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. Near interbank rate. The relationship just gets cleaner. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + escalating personal energy
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner buying from China. Your supplier is not your enemy. But they're also not protecting your margin. They quote in USD because you asked, and then they pad the price 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their end. 7 to 10 day wait at the bank. Forex fee on conversion. FX risk while the wire crosses oceans. None of it is your problem on paper. All of it is on your invoice. I see this every week. Importers wiring USD, supplier quietly collecting the buffer, importer wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter. Stop. Ask for CNY price. Send WorldFirst direct to a Chinese bank account. Same-day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst. Now.

They quote in USD because you asked, and then they often pad the price 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their end. 7 to 10 day wait at the bank. Forex fee on conversion. FX risk while the wire crosses oceans. None of it is your problem on paper. All of it is on your invoice. This shows up often. Importers wiring USD, supplier quietly collecting the buffer, importer wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter. Ask for the CNY price. Send via WorldFirst direct to a Chinese bank account. Same-day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here's something for every business owner buying from China to think about. Your supplier is not your enemy. We want to be clear on that. But they're also not in the business of protecting your margin. That's not their job. So what happens? They quote you in US dollars because that's what you asked for. And then they often pad the price by 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their side. Walk through what that means. The wire sits at their Chinese bank for 7 to 10 days. The bank charges a fee to change US dollars into Chinese yuan. The rate between the two currencies moves while the wire crosses oceans. None of that shows up on your invoice as a line item. But all of it is hiding inside the price you pay. This pattern shows up over and over. Importers wire USD. Supplier quietly collects the buffer. Importer keeps wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter, even when sourcing and orders look the same. Try this. Ask your supplier for the price in Chinese yuan on your next order. Then send the money via WorldFirst. We move Chinese yuan straight to your supplier's Chinese bank account. Same day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: Core mechanism (USD inbound delay, forex fee on conversion, supplier FX risk leading to padded quotes) is well-grounded in the findings. The 7 to 10 day wait and 2 to 5% padding figures are directly from the source brief. Main risk is the universal framing of 'every single time' and the implication that all suppliers consistently pad by this range. 'Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower' is presented as certainty when the source says 1 to 3% and 'often unlocks'. Soft brand-search close lowers overall risk.

skeptic_comments.text: Pad 2 to 5%? My supplier already quote me thin lah. Got no room to drop.
Eh I tried ask CNY price last time, same lor. Not lower.
Who is WorldFirst? Never see in Maybank app or anywhere.
Got Malaysia license? Or this Hong Kong company?
OK the 7 to 10 day wait part actually true. My supplier complain before.

PH2.analysis: Five-year compound (30,000 dollars on a 20K order base, 10 orders a year) is the highest-risk claim. It assumes a flat 3% padding holds across 50 orders, which a skeptic will not accept on faith. The 80% same-day CNY landing stat is from the source brief and defensible. 'No padding' as an outcome is an overreach, more honest framing is 'less padding'. 'Cleaner price every order' is a marketing claim.

skeptic_comments.text: 30K over 5 years? Bro you assume every order same size and same padding.
Where the 3% padding number from? Got source or just say?
I no do 10 orders a year leh. Small importer here.
80% same day to China sound nice. But which 80%, big banks or small?
Sound like ad. Show me one real before-after invoice please.

PH3.analysis: 'Lying to your face' was the original opener and the polished version softened it to 'hasn't told you something', which lowers the red flag substantially but still implies hidden bad faith from the supplier. 'They pocket it as a buffer' is true mechanically but loaded language. The internal price assumption (19,400 on a 20K quote) is hypothetical illustration not from a real case. 'Padding has no reason to stay' is conditional in source brief (suppliers OFTEN drop), framed as certain here.

skeptic_comments.text: My supplier no lie what. Just business. Both side want margin.
How you know internal price exactly 19,400? Made up number la.
Ask for CNY price means I cannot hold them to USD quote next time. Risky.
Near interbank rate, prove it. Show me a screenshot of WorldFirst quote.
What if supplier just say no CNY price? Then what?

PH4.analysis: Cleanest of the Hormozi set. Mechanism explanation is accurate to the source brief, with proper ranges (0.3 to 0.8% forex fee, 7 to 10 day wait, 2 to 5% padding) instead of single-point claims. Frames the supplier as solving a problem rather than as a bad actor, which is more accurate to the actual dynamic. 'No reason to stay on the next quote' is honest conditional language. Near interbank rate claim is defensible for WorldFirst's CIPS/MYbank routing.

skeptic_comments.text: OK the mechanism actually make sense, USD inbound at China bank is slow.
0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on their side, fair enough. Closer to reality.
But how WorldFirst send CNY direct? They got China bank license?
Near interbank rate, what does that mean for my MYR to CNY exactly?
Need to see WorldFirst rate vs Maybank rate side by side.

PH5.analysis: Three-things-stack format is clean and matches the source brief structure exactly. 'They carry risk' is more accurate than 'they gamble' from the original. The 'often drops 1 to 3%' framing is conditional and defensible. Main residual risk is 'no padding excuse' which is a confident outcome claim, and 'send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today' which a skeptic could pick at because CN-side bank can still apply small handling charges.

skeptic_comments.text: Three-thing breakdown clear. But 'they carry FX risk' my supplier hedge already.
Send 20K CNY today, supplier gets it today, sure or not? Holidays leh.
Worth a look, OK softer. But who actually WorldFirst? Ant Group I heard.
Quote drop 1 to 3% only? Not worth the switch if account opening hassle.
WorldFirst regulated by BNM ah? Or which authority covers MY users?

PG1.analysis: Softer opinion-led framing of the same supplier-perspective mechanism. 'Their headache eases' is more honest than 'disappears'. 'Padding has no reason to stay' is conditional and defensible. Main risk is 'they never did' want USD, which is opinion overstated as fact, but framed within Gary Vee voice which gives some license. The 2 to 5% range tracks the source brief. Brand-search close is soft.

skeptic_comments.text: Never wanted my USD? Some suppliers prefer USD for their own books actually.
Ask for CNY price, OK I can try. But what if quote stays same?
Same-day to China bank, proof? My friend's bank still 1 to 2 days.
Who is WorldFirst lah. Need to research first.
1 to 3% drop sound OK. But fees on WorldFirst side?

PG2.analysis: Compound math is more honest here (10 orders = ~6K, 20 = ~12K) and uses 'roughly' and 'around' qualifiers throughout, which is the polished improvement. 'More of the difference stays in your business' is the softened close vs original 'the buffer leaves' absolute. Still relies on the universal 3% padding assumption. 'Doesn't push back when you pay USD' is opinion-as-observation, defensible in voice.

skeptic_comments.text: Roughly 6K saved? Roughly means you not sure. Numbers feel soft now.
Supplier no push back on USD because what's the alternative for them?
Most importers miss this, OK fine. But I see plenty Reddit posts about it.
Near interbank rate, what's the actual spread vs my CIMB?
Account opening on WorldFirst, how long? KYC strict?

PG3.analysis: Softened from 'Stop quoting' to 'Rethink quoting' which lowers commanding tone. 'Often padded 2 to 5%' uses the conditional language properly. The hammer-wood-style metaphor is replaced with currency-choice framing which is cleaner. Main residual risk is 'commonly drop 1 to 3%' which is from the source brief but presented as if it always happens. The closer 'your margin is in the currency choice' is a confident claim defensible as opinion.

skeptic_comments.text: Suppliers commonly drop 1 to 3%, commonly means not always lah.
Currency choice = margin, OK clever. But really 1 to 3% on every order?
Switch from USD to CNY, what about my own books denominated in MYR?
Who is WorldFirst again? Show licence and credentials.
Near interbank rate for MYR to CNY direct? Or got intermediate USD step?

PG4.analysis: Conspiratorial frame softened from 'will never say to your face' to 'probably won't say openly' which lowers the red flag. 'They tolerate it' is accurate and defensible. 'Buffer often eases' is honest conditional language. 'Don't really prefer your USD' is opinion overstated, some suppliers genuinely do want USD for trade financing or domestic CNY weakness hedging, but the script doesn't acknowledge that. 'Relationship gets cleaner' is metaphor.

skeptic_comments.text: Some suppliers DO want USD though, for their own forex hedging.
Buffer often eases, OK careful wording. But by how much exactly?
Tolerate USD, fair point. My supplier always slow to confirm USD wire.
Relationship cleaner, nice line. But what if supplier prefer their USD account?
WorldFirst sends CNY direct, but from my MYR or via USD bridge?

PG5.analysis: Cleanest script in the set. Frames supplier as not-the-enemy which de-escalates conspiratorial tone. 'Not protecting your margin' is honest and defensible. Uses 'often pad' qualifier throughout. 'This shows up often' is softer than 'every week'. Brand integration is clean ('send via WorldFirst direct'). No compound math overreach, no universal claims, just the source-brief mechanism and a soft prompt to test the CNY price.

skeptic_comments.text: Supplier not protecting my margin, true lah. Why would they.
Often pad 2 to 5%, OK careful number. Reasonable.
Test the CNY price, fair experiment. Cost me nothing to ask.
Who is WorldFirst though? First time hearing. Got Malaysia office?
Near interbank rate from MYR side, what's the all-in vs Maybank?
